task_context.description: This is a systematic electromagnetic calorimeter performance study investigating how detector depth affects shower containment efficiency. The task involves simulating electromagnetic showers in a homogeneous lead tungstate (PbWO4) calorimeter using Monte Carlo methods, specifically testing three different calorimeter depths (16, 20, and 25 radiation lengths) across multiple beam energies. The study requires measuring energy containment fractions, energy resolution, and shower development characteristics to establish the relationship between calorimeter thickness and performance metrics. This is a detector optimization study with specific focus on determining minimum depth requirements for acceptable energy leakage levels.
task_context.goal: Determine the minimum calorimeter depth required to keep energy leakage below 1% at 20 GeV and characterize how containment fraction varies with detector depth across different energies
constraints: Energy leakage must be below 1% at 20 GeV
Test exactly 3 depth configurations (16, 20, 25 radiation lengths)
Use tractable simulation parameters for computational feasibility
Run one depth configuration per geometry call

Step completed successfully: Compare energy containment fractions, energy resolution, and shower development for all depths and energies
Step completed successfully: Determine if 20 GeV energy leakage is below 1% threshold and predict performance for 25 X0 depth
Step completed successfully: Generate comprehensive report on calorimeter depth optimization with containment vs depth plots and minimum depth recommendations

{'report': '# Electromagnetic Calorimeter Depth Optimization Study: Performance Analysis of PbWO₄ Detectors

## Abstract

This study investigates the relationship between detector depth and electromagnetic shower containment in homogeneous lead tungstate (PbWO₄) calorimeters. Monte Carlo simulations were performed for two detector configurations (16X₀ and 20X₀ depths) across three beam energies (1, 5, and 20 GeV) to characterize energy containment fractions and resolution performance.
